title: logos
text:
logos lets you prove properties of your Python code using a composable tactic system backed by a small trusted kernel. Write propositions as Python expressions, then discharge them with tactics — or with an LLM oracle whose output the kernel validates.
import logos
import logos.tactics as t

# Declare a theorem
@logos.theorem
def add_comm(x: int, y: int):
    return x + y == y + x

# Attach proof and validate
logos.check(add_comm.proof(t.intro("x", "y"), t.ring()))
print("Proved: addition is commutative")

Proved: addition is commutative
If the proof is wrong, LogosProofError is raised immediately. If it passes, add_comm.certified is True and you can build on it.

title: logos
text:
How it works
logos follows Lean’s architecture:
User layer       @logos.define · @logos.theorem · operator expressions
                           ↓
Tactic layer     ring · linarith · intro · cases · ...  (untrusted)
                           ↓
Proof terms      Refl · Trans · Subst · ForallIntro · ...
                           ↓
Kernel (~200 ln) infer() / check()  ←  the only trusted code
The kernel is the only trusted code (~200 lines). Tactics are arbitrary Python that produce proof terms — the kernel checks every term structurally. A tactic can even call an LLM: if the LLM produces an invalid proof, the kernel rejects it.

title: Knights and Knaves
text:
The classic puzzles of Raymond Smullyan: an island where every inhabitant is either a knight (always tells the truth) or a knave (always lies). Given what the inhabitants say, deduce who is who.
Logos can verify these deductions formally — you write the proof, logos checks it.
import logos
import logos.tactics as t

title: Knights and Knaves
text:
Encoding
Each inhabitant has two proposition variables:

a_kn — “A is a knight”
a_kv — “A is a knave”

Every puzzle rests on four structural constraints shared by all inhabitants:

Exhaustivity: everyone is a knight or a knave — a_kn | a_kv
Exclusivity: no one is both — a_kn >> ~a_kv, a_kv >> ~a_kn
Knight rule: if a person is a knight, their statement is true — a_kn >> <statement>
Knave rule: if a person is a knave, their statement is false — a_kv >> ~<statement>

Because Python’s >> is left-associative — A >> B >> C parses as (A >> B) >> C — theorems with many premises use logos.chain(h₁, h₂, ..., conclusion), which builds the correct right-associative chain h₁ >> (h₂ >> ...).

title: Knights and Knaves
text:
Puzzle 1 — “We Are Both Knaves”
A says: “We are both knaves.”
Reasoning. If A were a knight, the statement would be true — but then A would be a knave, a contradiction. So A must be a knave; the statement is false; not both are knaves; so B is a knight.

@logos.theorem
def puzzle1(a_kn: bool, a_kv: bool, b_kn: bool, b_kv: bool):
    return logos.chain(
        a_kn | a_kv,                  # A is knight or knave
        b_kn | b_kv,                  # B is knight or knave
        a_kn >> ~a_kv,                # exclusivity
        a_kv >> ~a_kn,
        b_kn >> ~b_kv,
        b_kv >> ~b_kn,
        a_kn >> (a_kv & b_kv),       # knight: "we're both knaves" is true
        a_kv >> ~(a_kv & b_kv),      # knave: it's false
        a_kv & b_kn,                  # conclusion: A=knave, B=knight
    )

Manual proof — explicit case analysis with forward reasoning:

logos.check(puzzle1.proof(
    t.intro("a_kn", "a_kv", "b_kn", "b_kv",     # introduce bool variables
            "h_a_ex", "h_b_ex",                   # exhaustivity premises
            "h_akn_exc", "h_akv_exc",             # exclusivity
            "h_bkn_exc", "h_bkv_exc",
            "h_kn_says", "h_kv_says"),            # A's knight/knave rules

    t.cases("h_a_ex", "h_a"),                     # split: is A a knight or knave?

    # ── Case A = knight  (h_a : a_kn) ──────────────────────────────
    [
        t.have("h_both_kv", "h_kn_says", "h_a"), # h_both_kv : a_kv & b_kv
        t.andL("h_both_kv", "h_akv"),             # h_akv     : a_kv
        t.have("h_not_akv", "h_akn_exc", "h_a"), # h_not_akv : ~a_kv
        t.contradiction(),                         # a_kv  and  ~a_kv
    ],

    # ── Case A = knave  (h_a : a_kv) ───────────────────────────────
    [
        t.have("h_not_both", "h_kv_says", "h_a"),# h_not_both : ~(a_kv & b_kv)
        t.cases("h_b_ex", "h_b"),                 # split on B's type

        # Sub-case B = knight  (h_b : b_kn)
        [
            t.split(),
            [t.assumption()],   # a_kv  (= h_a)
            [t.assumption()],   # b_kn  (= h_b)
        ],

        # Sub-case B = knave  (h_b : b_kv)
        [
            t.conj("h_a", "h_b", "h_both"),      # h_both : a_kv & b_kv
            t.contradiction(),                     # ~(a_kv & b_kv) and  a_kv & b_kv
        ],
    ],
))
print("Puzzle 1: A=knave, B=knight ✓")

Puzzle 1: A=knave, B=knight ✓


The tactics used here are all forward-reasoning primitives:







Tactic
What it does




t.cases(h, name)
Split h : A \| B; subgoal 1 gets name : A, subgoal 2 gets name : B


t.have(new, h_impl, h_ant)
Modus ponens: from h_impl : A→B and h_ant : A, add new : B


t.andL(h, name)
From h : A & B, extract left component into name : A


t.andR(h, name)
From h : A & B, extract right component into name : B


t.conj(h1, h2, name)
From h1 : A and h2 : B, add name : A & B


t.contradiction()
Close the goal when context contains P and ~P, ~(A\|B) with A or B present, or ~(A&B) with both A and B present

title: Knights and Knaves
section: Puzzle 2 — “At Least One of Us Is a Knave”
text:
Puzzle 2 — “At Least One of Us Is a Knave”
A says: “At least one of us is a knave.”
Reasoning. If A were a knave, the statement “at least one is a knave” would be false — so neither would be a knave. But that contradicts A being a knave. So A is a knight. The statement is true: a_kv | b_kv. Since A is a knight, ~a_kv, so B must be the knave.

@logos.theorem
def puzzle2(a_kn: bool, a_kv: bool, b_kn: bool, b_kv: bool):
    return logos.chain(
        a_kn | a_kv,
        b_kn | b_kv,
        a_kn >> ~a_kv,
        a_kv >> ~a_kn,
        b_kn >> ~b_kv,
        b_kv >> ~b_kn,
        a_kn >> (a_kv | b_kv),       # knight: "at least one is a knave" is true
        a_kv >> ~(a_kv | b_kv),      # knave: it's false — both would be knights
        a_kn & b_kv,                  # conclusion: A=knight, B=knave
    )

This time logos.prove() finds the proof automatically:

logos.prove(puzzle2)
print("Puzzle 2: A=knight, B=knave ✓  (auto)")

Puzzle 2: A=knight, B=knave ✓  (auto)


The auto tactic explores the proof space algorithmically at each step:

Introduces all universally-quantified variables and premises into context
Splits the conjunction goal (a_kn & b_kv) into two independent subgoals
Case-analyses every disjunctive (|) hypothesis in context
Applies forward reasoning — if H : A→B and A is known, derives B
Closes with contradiction (including ~(A|B) with A present) or assumption

title: Knights and Knaves
text:
Puzzle 3 — Three People
A says: “B is a knave.” B says: “A and C are the same type” (both knights or both knaves). C says: “A is a knight.”
Reasoning.

If A = knight: A says B=knave → B=knave. B=knave → B’s statement is false → A and C are different types → C=knave (since A=knight). C=knave → C’s statement is false → A=knave. Contradiction.
If A = knave: A says B=knave → false → B=knight. B=knight → B’s statement is true: A and C are the same type. A=knave, so C=knave. C=knave → C’s statement is false → A is not a knight. Consistent ✓

Conclusion: A=knave, B=knight, C=knave.

@logos.theorem
def puzzle3(a_kn: bool, a_kv: bool,
            b_kn: bool, b_kv: bool,
            c_kn: bool, c_kv: bool):
    b_says = (a_kn & c_kn) | (a_kv & c_kv)   # "A and C are the same type"
    return logos.chain(
        a_kn | a_kv,                 # exhaustivity
        b_kn | b_kv,
        c_kn | c_kv,
        a_kn >> ~a_kv,               # exclusivity
        a_kv >> ~a_kn,
        b_kn >> ~b_kv,
        b_kv >> ~b_kn,
        c_kn >> ~c_kv,
        c_kv >> ~c_kn,
        a_kn >> b_kv,                # A=knight: "B is knave" is true
        a_kv >> b_kn,                # A=knave:  "B is knave" is false
        b_kn >> b_says,              # B=knight: "A,C same type" is true
        b_kv >> ~b_says,             # B=knave:  "A,C same type" is false
        c_kn >> a_kn,                # C=knight: "A is knight" is true
        c_kv >> ~a_kn,               # C=knave:  "A is knight" is false
        a_kv & b_kn & c_kv,         # conclusion: A=knave, B=knight, C=knave
    )


logos.check(puzzle3.proof(
    t.intro("a_kn", "a_kv", "b_kn", "b_kv", "c_kn", "c_kv",
            "h_a_ex", "h_b_ex", "h_c_ex",
            "h_akn_exc", "h_akv_exc",
            "h_bkn_exc", "h_bkv_exc",
            "h_ckn_exc", "h_ckv_exc",
            "h_a_kn_says", "h_a_kv_says",
            "h_b_kn_says", "h_b_kv_says",
            "h_c_kn_says", "h_c_kv_says"),

    t.cases("h_a_ex", "h_a"),

    # ── Case A = knight  (h_a : a_kn) ──────────────────────────────────
    # A=knight → B=knave.  B=knave → b_says is false.
    # In either sub-case for C, we reach a contradiction.
    [
        t.have("h_bkv",      "h_a_kn_says", "h_a"),    # B is knave
        t.have("h_not_bsays","h_b_kv_says", "h_bkv"),  # ~((a_kn&c_kn)|(a_kv&c_kv))
        t.cases("h_c_ex", "h_c"),

        # Sub-case C = knight  (h_c : c_kn)
        # a_kn & c_kn satisfies the left disjunct of b_says → contradicts ~b_says
        [
            t.conj("h_a", "h_c", "h_ac"),    # h_ac : a_kn & c_kn
            t.contradiction(),                 # ~(A|B) with A present
        ],

        # Sub-case C = knave  (h_c : c_kv)
        # C=knave says "A is knight" is false → ~a_kn, contradicts h_a : a_kn
        [
            t.have("h_not_akn", "h_c_kv_says", "h_c"),
            t.contradiction(),                 # a_kn and ~a_kn
        ],
    ],

    # ── Case A = knave  (h_a : a_kv) ───────────────────────────────────
    # A=knave → B=knight.  B=knight → b_says is true.
    # C=knight sub-case leads to contradiction; C=knave is the solution.
    [
        t.have("h_bkn", "h_a_kv_says", "h_a"),    # B is knight
        t.cases("h_c_ex", "h_c"),

        # Sub-case C = knight  (h_c : c_kn)
        # C=knight says A=knight → a_kn.  But h_a says a_kv.
        # Exclusivity: a_kv → ~a_kn.  So a_kn and ~a_kn.
        [
            t.have("h_akn",     "h_c_kn_says", "h_c"),    # a_kn
            t.have("h_not_akn", "h_akv_exc",   "h_a"),    # ~a_kn
            t.contradiction(),
        ],

        # Sub-case C = knave  (h_c : c_kv)  — the solution
        [
            t.split(),
            [t.split(), [t.assumption()], [t.assumption()]],  # a_kv & b_kn
            [t.assumption()],                                   # c_kv
        ],
    ],
))
print("Puzzle 3: A=knave, B=knight, C=knave ✓")

Puzzle 3: A=knave, B=knight, C=knave ✓

title: Defining Functions
text: @logos.define is how you introduce functions into the logos proof system. The function body runs in expression-tree mode — operators build trees instead of evaluating — and the result is a Function object you can use in proofs.

title: Defining Functions
Defining Functions
text:
Dual Mode
The same function works as a normal Python function and as a symbolic expression builder:

x = logos.var("x", int)
from logos.expr import App

# Python mode — called with a concrete value
print(double(5))    # 10 (= 5 + 5)
print(double(7))    # 14

# Symbolic mode — called with a Var
result = double(x)
print(type(result).__name__)   # App
print(result.func_name)        # double
print(result.args)             # [Var("x", int)]

10
14
App
double
[Var(name='x', type=<class 'int'>)]

title: Defining Functions
text:
Proving About Defined Functions
Use t.unfold(double) (the Function object) to replace double(x) with its definition body:

@logos.theorem
def double_is_2x(x: int):
    return double(x) == logos.lit(2) * x

logos.check(double_is_2x.proof(t.intro("x"), t.unfold(double), t.ring()))
print("Proved: double(x) = 2x")

Proved: double(x) = 2x


The proof trace is: 1. intro("x") — introduce x from the Forall 2. unfold(double) — replaces double(x) with x + x in the goal 3. ring() — proves x + x == 2 * x by polynomial normalization

title: Defining Functions
Defining Functions
Defining Functions
text:
External (Black-Box) Functions
For functions logos can’t inspect — C extensions, external APIs, database queries — declare them symbolically with logos.extern and axiomatize their properties:

# Declare an external sorting function
sorted_fn = logos.extern("my_sorted", [list], list)

xs = logos.var("xs", list)

# State what we know about it
@logos.axiom
def sorted_returns_same_type(xs: list):
    return sorted_fn(xs) == sorted_fn(xs)

# Now we can use sorted_fn in propositions and proofs
from logos.expr import App
result = sorted_fn(xs)
print(type(result).__name__)  # App
print(result.func_name)       # my_sorted

App
my_sorted








Note



The extern approach is how logos reasons about library functions you can’t redefine. You state the properties you need as axioms, then build proofs on top of them.

title: Defining Functions
text:
Multiple Parameters
Definitions can take any number of parameters:

@logos.define
def clamp(x: int, lo: int, hi: int):
    return logos.cases(
        (x < lo, lo),
        (x > hi, hi),
        (logos.lit(True), x),   # default/else case
    )

print(clamp(-5, 0, 10))   # 0
print(clamp(50, 0, 10))   # 10
print(clamp(5, 0, 10))    # 5

CaseExpr(branches=[(True, 0), (False, 10), (Lit(value=True), -5)])
CaseExpr(branches=[(False, 0), (True, 10), (Lit(value=True), 50)])
CaseExpr(branches=[(False, 0), (False, 10), (Lit(value=True), 5)])

title: Tactics Overview
text: A tactic is a Python function that transforms a proof goal. Tactics are untrusted — they produce proof terms, and the kernel validates every term before the proof is accepted.

title: Tactics Overview
text:
The Goal
Every tactic receives a Goal object:

import logos
import logos.tactics as t
from logos.goal import Goal

x = logos.var("x", int)
goal = Goal(
    context={"h": x > logos.lit(0)},    # hypotheses in scope
    statement=x > logos.lit(0),          # what to prove
)
print("Statement:", goal.statement)
print("Context:", goal.context)

Statement: Gt(left=Var(name='x', type=<class 'int'>), right=Lit(value=0))
Context: {'h': Gt(left=Var(name='x', type=<class 'int'>), right=Lit(value=0))}

title: Tactics Overview
text:
Tactic Protocol
A tactic is a callable with signature:
Goal → ProofTerm                          # closes the goal
Goal → (list[Goal], compose_fn)           # splits into subgoals

Closing: returns a ProofTerm directly. The proof is complete.
Splitting: returns (subgoals, compose_fn). Each subgoal must be proved; compose_fn assembles the final proof from the subgoal proofs.

title: Tactics Overview
text:
The Tactic Runner
logos.runner.run(goal, by) drives the tactic list:

from logos.runner import run, TacticFailed
from logos.goal import Goal
from logos.helpers import structural_eq

x = logos.var("x", int)

# A goal: prove x > 0 given h: x > 0
goal = Goal({"h": x > logos.lit(0)}, x > logos.lit(0))
proof = run(goal, [t.assumption()])
print("Proof term type:", type(proof).__name__)  # HypRef

Proof term type: HypRef

title: Tactics Overview
text:
Nested Lists for Subgoals
When a tactic produces multiple subgoals, wrap each subgoal’s tactics in a nested list [...]:

from logos.expr import And

x, y = logos.vars("x y", int)
goal = Goal(
    {"hx": x > logos.lit(0), "hy": y > logos.lit(0)},
    (x > logos.lit(0)) & (y > logos.lit(0)),
)

proof = run(goal, [
    t.split(),            # produces 2 subgoals
    [t.assumption()],     # handles subgoal 1: x > 0
    [t.assumption()],     # handles subgoal 2: y > 0
])
print("Proved:", type(proof).__name__)   # AndIntro

Proved: AndIntro

title: Tactics Overview
text:
Tactic Categories







Category
Tactics




Structural
intro, assumption, exact, apply, split, left, right, witness, contradiction


Arithmetic
ring, linarith, decide


Rewriting
unfold, refl, rewrite, rewrite_rev, eval_, norm_num


Combinators
then, first, try_, repeat, all_goals



See the sub-pages for detailed documentation on each category.

title: Tactics Overview
text:
Writing Your Own Tactic
Any Python function that follows the protocol is a valid tactic:

from logos.goal import Goal
from logos.kernel import Refl
from logos.helpers import structural_eq
from logos.runner import TacticFailed
from logos.expr import Eq

def my_refl(goal: Goal):
    """Proves a == a by reflexivity."""
    match goal.statement:
        case Eq(lhs, rhs) if structural_eq(lhs, rhs):
            return Refl(lhs)
        case _:
            raise TacticFailed("my_refl: goal is not a == a")

x = logos.var("x", int)
goal = Goal({}, x == x)
proof = run(goal, [my_refl])
print("Custom tactic worked:", type(proof).__name__)  # Refl

Custom tactic worked: Refl


See Writing Custom Tactics → for advanced patterns.

title: Structural Tactics
text:
Structural tactics manipulate the shape of the goal — introducing variables, splitting conjunctions, pattern-matching on disjunctions.
import logos
import logos.tactics as t
from logos.goal import Goal
from logos.runner import run
from logos.kernel import infer
from logos.helpers import structural_eq

title: Structural Tactics
text:
intro(*names)
Strips leading Forall and Implies from the goal, adding the introduced items to the context.

x, y = logos.vars("x y", int)

# Introduce universally quantified variables
logos.check(logos.prove(
    logos.forall(int, int, lambda x, y: x + y == y + x),
    [
        t.intro("x", "y"),   # strips Forall(x, Forall(y, ...)), adds x,y to context
        t.ring(),
    ],
))
print("Proved with intro")

# Introduce hypothesis from implication
logos.check(logos.prove(
    logos.forall(int, lambda x: (x > logos.lit(0)) >> (x > logos.lit(0))),
    [
        t.intro("x", "h"),   # x from Forall, h: x>0 from Implies
        t.assumption(),       # close with h
    ],
))
print("Proved implication with intro")

Proved with intro
Proved implication with intro

title: Structural Tactics
text:
assumption()
Closes the goal if it appears verbatim in the context.

x = logos.var("x", int)
hyp = x > logos.lit(0)

goal = Goal({"h": hyp}, hyp)
proof = run(goal, [t.assumption()])
print("Closed by assumption:", type(proof).__name__)  # HypRef

Closed by assumption: HypRef

title: Structural Tactics
text:
exact(term)
Closes the goal with an explicit proof term. For when you know exactly what kernel term to supply.

from logos.kernel import Refl

x = logos.var("x", int)
goal = Goal({}, x == x)
proof = run(goal, [t.exact(Refl(x))])
print("Closed by exact:", type(proof).__name__)  # Refl

Closed by exact: Refl

title: Structural Tactics
text:
split()
For conjunction goals A & B — splits into two subgoals, one for A and one for B.

x, y = logos.vars("x y", int)

logos.check(logos.prove(
    logos.forall(int, int, lambda x, y:
        (x > logos.lit(0)) >> ((y > logos.lit(0)) >> ((x > logos.lit(0)) & (y > logos.lit(0))))
    ),
    [
        t.intro("x", "y", "hx", "hy"),
        t.split(),
        [t.assumption()],   # prove x > 0
        [t.assumption()],   # prove y > 0
    ],
))
print("Proved conjunction")

Proved conjunction

title: Structural Tactics
text:
left(right_type) / right(left_type)
For disjunction goals A | B — choose which side to prove.

x = logos.var("x", int)

# Prove "x > 0 or x > 0" by proving the left side
logos.check(logos.prove(
    logos.forall(int, lambda x:
        (x > logos.lit(0)) >> ((x > logos.lit(0)) | (x > logos.lit(0)))
    ),
    [
        t.intro("x", "h"),
        t.left(x > logos.lit(0)),   # choose to prove left side; right_type = x > 0
        t.assumption(),
    ],
))
print("Proved disjunction via left")

Proved disjunction via left

title: Structural Tactics
text:
witness(val)
For existential goals Exists(x, P(x)) — provide the witness value, reducing to P[x:=val].

logos.check(logos.prove(
    logos.exists(int, lambda x: x > logos.lit(5)),
    [
        t.witness(logos.lit(10)),   # instantiate x := 10
        t.decide(),                  # verify 10 > 5
    ],
))
print("Proved: ∃x > 5  (witness: 10)")

Proved: ∃x > 5  (witness: 10)

title: Structural Tactics
text:
contradiction()
Closes the goal if the context contains both P and ~P for some proposition P.

from logos.expr import Not

x = logos.var("x", int)
p = x > logos.lit(0)

# Context has both h: x > 0 and hn: ~(x > 0) — contradiction!
goal = Goal({"h": p, "hn": Not(p)}, x == logos.lit(99))
proof = run(goal, [t.contradiction()])
print("Closed by contradiction:", type(proof).__name__)  # ExFalso

Closed by contradiction: ExFalso

title: Structural Tactics
text:
apply(ref)
Applies an Axiom/Theorem object (or a local hypothesis string) whose statement matches the current goal.

x, y = logos.vars("x y", int)

# Declare and prove a theorem
@logos.theorem
def x_eq_x(x: int):
    return x == x

logos.check(x_eq_x.proof(t.intro("x"), t.refl()))

# Apply it inside another proof using the object
x_inst = logos.var("x", int)
goal = Goal({}, x_inst == x_inst)
proof = run(goal, [t.apply(x_eq_x)])
print("Applied theorem:", type(proof).__name__)

Applied theorem: ForallElim


You can also apply local hypotheses by name:

x = logos.var("x", int)
hyp = x > logos.lit(0)
goal = Goal({"h": hyp}, hyp)
proof = run(goal, [t.apply("h")])
print("Applied hypothesis:", type(proof).__name__)

Applied hypothesis: HypRef

title: Rewrite Tactics
text:
Rewrite tactics transform the goal by unfolding definitions, applying equational lemmas, or evaluating sub-expressions.
import logos
import logos.tactics as t

title: Rewrite Tactics
text:
refl()
Closes a == a goals — when both sides are structurally identical.

x = logos.var("x", int)

logos.check(logos.prove(logos.forall(int, lambda x: x == x),
            [t.intro("x"), t.refl()]))
print("x = x ✓")

# Also works on expressions
logos.check(logos.prove(
    logos.forall(int, int, lambda x, y: x + y == x + y),
    [t.intro("x", "y"), t.refl()],
))
print("x + y = x + y ✓")

x = x ✓
x + y = x + y ✓


refl fails when the sides differ — even if they’re equivalent:

from logos.theorem import LogosProofError

try:
    logos.check(logos.prove(logos.forall(int, int, lambda x, y: x + y == y + x),
                [t.intro("x", "y"), t.refl()]))
except LogosProofError:
    print("refl cannot prove x + y = y + x (use ring instead)")

refl cannot prove x + y = y + x (use ring instead)

title: Rewrite Tactics
Rewrite Tactics
text:
rewrite(ref) / rewrite_rev(ref)
Rewrites the goal using an Axiom or Theorem object. rewrite applies left-to-right; rewrite_rev applies right-to-left.

x, y = logos.vars("x y", int)

# Prove a useful lemma
@logos.theorem
def triple_def(x: int):
    return triple(x) == x + x + x

logos.check(triple_def.proof(t.intro("x"), t.unfold(triple), t.refl()))

# Use it to rewrite inside another goal
logos.check(logos.prove(
    logos.forall(int, lambda x: triple(x) + x == logos.lit(4) * x),
    [
        t.intro("x"),
        t.rewrite(triple_def),   # replaces triple(x) with x+x+x
        t.ring(),
    ],
))
print("Proved via rewrite")

Proved via rewrite

title: Rewrite Tactics
text:
eval_()
Evaluates ground arithmetic sub-expressions (no variables) within the goal.

logos.check(logos.prove(
    logos.lit(2) + logos.lit(3) == logos.lit(5),
    [t.eval_()],
))
print("2 + 3 = 5 ✓ (via eval)")

logos.check(logos.prove(
    logos.lit(10) - logos.lit(4) == logos.lit(6),
    [t.eval_()],
))
print("10 - 4 = 6 ✓")

2 + 3 = 5 ✓ (via eval)
10 - 4 = 6 ✓

title: Rewrite Tactics
text:
norm_num()
Normalizes numeric expressions and closes numeric ground goals. Similar to eval_ but more aggressive:

logos.check(logos.prove(logos.lit(6) == logos.lit(2) * logos.lit(3), [t.norm_num()]))
print("6 = 2 * 3 ✓")

logos.check(logos.prove(logos.lit(100) == logos.lit(10) ** logos.lit(2), [t.norm_num()]))
print("100 = 10² ✓")

6 = 2 * 3 ✓
100 = 10² ✓

title: Rewrite Tactics
Expression Language
text: Every logos proposition is built from expression trees. Instead of evaluating immediately, operators on Var and Expr objects build a tree that the kernel can inspect and manipulate symbolically.

title: Expression Language
text:
Variables and Literals

import logos
from logos.expr import Var, Lit

# Create variables — the building blocks of symbolic expressions
x = logos.var("x", int)
y = logos.var("y", int)

# Or several at once
a, b, c = logos.vars("a b c", int)

print(type(x))    # <class 'logos.expr.Var'>
print(x.name)     # x
print(x.type)     # <class 'int'>

# Literals are created by Lit() or auto-lifted from Python values
from logos.expr import Lit
zero = Lit(0)
print(zero)       # Lit(value=0)

<class 'logos.expr.Var'>
x
<class 'int'>
Lit(value=0)

title: Expression Language
text:
Arithmetic Operators
All Python arithmetic operators are overloaded to return Expr nodes rather than values. The right-hand side is auto-lifted from Python literals.

x, y = logos.vars("x y", int)

from logos.expr import Add, Sub, Mul, Neg, Lit

e1 = x + y         # Add(x, y)
e2 = x + 1         # Add(x, Lit(1))  ← literal auto-lifted
e3 = 2 * x         # Mul(Lit(2), x)  ← __rmul__
e4 = -x            # Neg(x)
e5 = x ** 2        # Pow(x, Lit(2))
e6 = x // y        # Div(x, y)
e7 = x % 3         # Mod(x, Lit(3))

print(type(e1).__name__)  # Add
print(type(e2).__name__)  # Add
print(e2.right)           # Lit(value=1)

Add
Add
Lit(value=1)

title: Expression Language
text:
Comparison Operators
Comparisons return Expr[bool] nodes — not Python booleans. This is the key design decision: x == y builds an Eq node, not True or False.

from logos.expr import Eq, Lt, Le, Gt, Ge

eq_expr  = x == y    # Eq(x, y)
lt_expr  = x < 0     # Lt(x, Lit(0))
le_expr  = x <= y    # Le(x, y)
gt_expr  = x > 1     # Gt(x, Lit(1))
ge_expr  = x >= 0    # Ge(x, Lit(0))

print(type(eq_expr).__name__)   # Eq
print(type(gt_expr).__name__)   # Gt

Eq
Gt

title: Expression Language
text:
Logical Connectives
Python’s and, or, and not can’t be overloaded (they’re keywords with short-circuit evaluation). Instead, use &, |, ~, and >>.

from logos.expr import And, Or, Not, Implies

p = x > logos.lit(0)
q = y > logos.lit(0)

conj    = p & q          # And(p, q)
disj    = p | q          # Or(p, q)
neg     = ~p             # Not(p)
impl    = p >> q         # Implies(p, q)

print(type(conj).__name__)   # And
print(type(impl).__name__)   # Implies

And
Implies

title: Expression Language
text:
The __bool__ Guard
If an Expr accidentally ends up in a Python if or assert, you get a clear error:

import pytest

try:
    if x > 0:   # ← this would call bool() on an Expr
        pass
except TypeError as e:
    print(f"Caught: {e}")

Caught: Expr cannot be used as a Python bool. Use & instead of 'and', | instead of 'or', ~ instead of 'not'.


This protects you from silent bugs where symbolic expressions are accidentally evaluated as Python booleans.

title: Expression Language
text:
Expression Trees Are Identity-Based
Two expression objects are never == in Python (that returns an Eq node). Identity comparison uses is or id(). Structural equality is handled by logos.helpers.structural_eq:

from logos.helpers import structural_eq

e1 = x + y
e2 = x + y   # different Python objects, same structure

print(e1 == e2)                  # Eq(Add(x,y), Add(x,y)) — NOT bool!
print(structural_eq(e1, e2))     # True  — structural comparison
print(e1 is e2)                  # False — different objects

Eq(left=Add(left=Var(name='x', type=<class 'int'>), right=Var(name='y', type=<class 'int'>)), right=Add(left=Var(name='x', type=<class 'int'>), right=Var(name='y', type=<class 'int'>)))
True
False

title: Expression Language
text:
Substitution
substitute(expr, var, val) replaces all free occurrences of a variable:

from logos.helpers import substitute

e = x + y
result = substitute(e, x, logos.lit(5))
print(structural_eq(result, logos.lit(5) + y))  # True

True


Substitution is capture-avoiding — it won’t replace a variable that’s bound inside a Forall:

from logos.expr import Forall

# Forall(x, x + y) — x is bound, y is free
e = Forall(x, x + y)
result = substitute(e, x, logos.lit(99))
# x inside the Forall is not replaced
print(structural_eq(result, e))  # True — substitution stopped at the binder

True

title: Propositions & Quantifiers
text: A proposition in logos is any Expr that represents a truth claim — typically an equation, inequality, or logical combination. Quantifiers let you make universal and existential claims.

title: Propositions & Quantifiers
text:
Universal Quantification
Forall says “for all values of these variables, this holds.”

import logos
import logos.tactics as t
from logos.expr import Var, Forall

x, y, z = logos.vars("x y z", int)

# Lambda form — most ergonomic
p1 = logos.forall(int, int, lambda x, y: x + y == y + x)

# Explicit form — useful when you have vars already
p2 = Forall(x, y, x + y == y + x)

# Nested Forall is sugar for multiple binders
p3 = logos.forall(int, int, int, lambda x, y, z: (x + y) + z == x + (y + z))

from logos.helpers import structural_eq
print(structural_eq(p1, p2))   # True — same structure

True

title: Propositions & Quantifiers
text:
Existential Quantification
Exists says “there is some value for which this holds.”

from logos.expr import Exists

# There exists an integer greater than 5
p = logos.exists(int, lambda x: x > logos.lit(5))

# Prove it by providing a witness
gp = logos.prove(
    logos.exists(int, lambda x: x > logos.lit(5)),
    [t.witness(logos.lit(10)), t.decide()],
)
logos.check(gp)
print("Proved: ∃x > 5")

Proved: ∃x > 5

title: Propositions & Quantifiers
text:
Implications
The >> operator builds implications. intro in a proof strips the implication and adds the antecedent as a hypothesis:

x = logos.var("x", int)

# If x > 0, then x + 1 > 0
p = (x > logos.lit(0)) >> (x + logos.lit(1) > logos.lit(0))

gp = logos.prove(
    logos.forall(int, lambda x: (x > logos.lit(0)) >> (x + logos.lit(1) > logos.lit(0))),
    [t.intro("x", "h"), t.linarith()],
)
logos.check(gp)
print("Proved: x > 0 → x + 1 > 0")

Proved: x > 0 → x + 1 > 0

title: Propositions & Quantifiers
text:
Conjunctions and Disjunctions

x, y = logos.vars("x y", int)

# Prove both sides independently
# Note: >> is left-associative in Python, so parenthesize for right-nesting
gp = logos.prove(
    logos.forall(int, int, lambda x, y:
        (x > logos.lit(0)) >> ((y > logos.lit(0)) >> ((x > logos.lit(0)) & (y > logos.lit(0))))
    ),
    [
        t.intro("x", "y", "hx", "hy"),
        t.split(),
        [t.assumption()],
        [t.assumption()],
    ],
)
logos.check(gp)
print("Proved: hx ∧ hy")

Proved: hx ∧ hy

title: Propositions & Quantifiers
Propositions & Quantifiers
Proving Theorems
text: @logos.theorem, logos.check, and logos.prove are the top-level entry points for proof checking.

title: Proving Theorems
Proving Theorems
text:
.proof(...) — attach a proof
Attach the proof spec with .proof(tactic1, tactic2, ...). Returns self for chaining:

# Chain style
logos.check(add_zero.proof(t.intro("x"), t.ring()))
print(add_zero.certified)   # True

True


Or use the decorator form:

@logos.theorem
def add_zero_v2(x: int):
    return x + logos.lit(0) == x

@add_zero_v2.proof
def _():
    return [t.intro("x"), t.ring()]

logos.check(add_zero_v2)
print("Proved with decorator style")

Proved with decorator style

title: Proving Theorems
text:
logos.check — the compute boundary
logos.check(thm) runs the kernel. Nothing executes until this call:

@logos.theorem
def mul_comm(x: int, y: int):
    return x * y == y * x

mul_comm.proof(t.intro("x", "y"), t.ring())   # just attaches, doesn't run

logos.check(mul_comm)   # NOW it runs
print(mul_comm.certified)   # True

True

title: Proving Theorems
Proving Theorems
section: What Happens When a Proof Fails

title: Proving Theorems
text:
logos.prove — one-shot anonymous proof
logos.prove(statement, proof_list) creates a _GroundProof — pass it to logos.check():

result = logos.check(logos.prove(
    logos.forall(int, lambda x: x + logos.lit(0) == x),
    [t.intro("x"), t.ring()],
))
print(result.certified)  # True

True

title: Proving Theorems
Proving Theorems
Proving Theorems
text:
Module-Level Theorem Files
A common pattern is a dedicated theorem file that’s imported wherever needed. Since logos.check raises LogosProofError at check time if a proof is wrong, it acts like a compile-time check:
# theorems/arithmetic.py
import logos
import logos.tactics as t

@logos.theorem
def add_comm(x: int, y: int):
    return x + y == y + x

logos.check(add_comm.proof(t.intro("x", "y"), t.ring()))

@logos.theorem
def mul_comm(x: int, y: int):
    return x * y == y * x

logos.check(mul_comm.proof(t.intro("x", "y"), t.ring()))

# If any proof breaks, this module fails to import.

title: Writing Custom Tactics
text:
Any Python function that follows the tactic protocol is a valid tactic. This page shows you how to build your own — from simple one-liners to full automation.
import logos
import logos.tactics as t
from logos.goal import Goal
from logos.runner import run, TacticFailed
from logos.kernel import Refl, ProofTerm, AndIntro
from logos.helpers import structural_eq

title: Writing Custom Tactics
text:
The Protocol
A tactic is a callable with one of two return shapes:
# Close the goal — return a proof term
def my_tactic(goal: Goal) -> ProofTerm:
    ...

# Split into subgoals — return (subgoals, compose_fn)
def my_tactic(goal: Goal) -> tuple:
    subgoals = [...]
    compose = lambda proofs: ...
    return subgoals, compose
Raise TacticFailed for any situation where the tactic can’t proceed.

title: Writing Custom Tactics
text:
A Closing Tactic: my_refl
The simplest tactic: close a == a by reflexivity.

from logos.expr import Eq

def my_refl(goal: Goal) -> ProofTerm:
    match goal.statement:
        case Eq(lhs, rhs) if structural_eq(lhs, rhs):
            return Refl(lhs)
        case _:
            raise TacticFailed(f"my_refl: expected a == a, got {goal.statement!r}")

x = logos.var("x", int)
goal = Goal({}, x == x)
proof = run(goal, [my_refl])
print("Custom refl:", type(proof).__name__)  # Refl

Custom refl: Refl

title: Writing Custom Tactics
text:
A Splitting Tactic: my_split
A splitting tactic returns (subgoals, compose_fn):

from logos.expr import And
from logos.kernel import AndIntro

def my_split(goal: Goal):
    match goal.statement:
        case And(left, right):
            subgoals = [
                Goal(goal.context, left),
                Goal(goal.context, right),
            ]
            compose = lambda proofs: AndIntro(proofs[0], proofs[1])
            return subgoals, compose
        case _:
            raise TacticFailed("my_split: expected A & B")

x, y = logos.vars("x y", int)
goal = Goal({"hx": x == x, "hy": y == y}, (x == x) & (y == y))

proof = run(goal, [
    my_split,
    [t.assumption()],
    [t.assumption()],
])
print("Custom split:", type(proof).__name__)  # AndIntro

Custom split: AndIntro

title: Writing Custom Tactics
Writing Custom Tactics
text:
A Transforming Tactic: normalize_goal
A tactic that rewrites the goal and returns a single new subgoal:

from logos.helpers import substitute
from logos.expr import Var, Lit

def simplify_double(goal: Goal):
    """Replace `x + x` with `2 * x` in the goal."""
    from logos.expr import Add, Mul
    def _simplify(expr):
        match expr:
            case Add(lhs, rhs) if structural_eq(lhs, rhs):
                return Mul(Lit(2), lhs)
            case _:
                cls = type(expr)
                if hasattr(cls, "__dataclass_fields__"):
                    fields = [getattr(expr, f) for f in cls.__dataclass_fields__]
                    new_fields = [_simplify(f) if hasattr(f, "__dataclass_fields__") or isinstance(f, type(logos.var("_", int))) else f for f in fields]
                    return cls(*new_fields)
                return expr

    new_stmt = _simplify(goal.statement)
    new_goal = Goal(goal.context, new_stmt)
    return [new_goal], lambda proofs: proofs[0]

title: Writing Custom Tactics
text:
Debugging Tactics: trace
A utility tactic that prints the current goal without changing it:

def trace(label: str = ""):
    """Print the current goal for debugging."""
    def tactic(goal: Goal):
        print(f"[trace{' '+label if label else ''}] Context: {list(goal.context.keys())}")
        print(f"[trace{' '+label if label else ''}] Goal: {goal.statement!r}")
        # Return a no-op: 1 subgoal (same goal), identity compose
        return [goal], lambda proofs: proofs[0]
    return tactic

x = logos.var("x", int)
logos.check(logos.prove(
    logos.forall(int, lambda x: x + logos.lit(0) == x),
    [
        trace("before intro"),
        t.intro("x"),
        trace("after intro"),
        t.ring(),
    ],
))

[trace before intro] Context: []
[trace before intro] Goal: ForallNode(var=Var(name='x', type=<class 'int'>), body=Eq(left=Add(left=Var(name='x', type=<class 'int'>), right=Lit(value=0)), right=Var(name='x', type=<class 'int'>)))
[trace after intro] Context: ['x']
[trace after intro] Goal: Eq(left=Add(left=Var(name='x', type=<class 'int'>), right=Lit(value=0)), right=Var(name='x', type=<class 'int'>))


<logos.theorem._GroundProof at 0x7fddacfa1880>

title: Writing Custom Tactics
Writing Custom Tactics
text:
Introspecting the Context
Custom tactics can read the context to make decisions:

def find_equality(goal: Goal):
    """Look for an equality hypothesis and try to rewrite with it."""
    from logos.expr import Eq
    for name, hyp in goal.context.items():
        match hyp:
            case Eq(lhs, rhs):
                # Found one — try rewriting
                from logos.tactics.rewrite import _replace_unify
                new_stmt = _replace_unify(goal.statement, lhs, rhs, set())
                if not structural_eq(new_stmt, goal.statement):
                    new_goal = Goal(goal.context, new_stmt)
                    return [new_goal], lambda ps: ps[0]
    raise TacticFailed("find_equality: no usable equality in context")

x = logos.var("x", int)
eq_hyp = x == logos.lit(5)
goal = Goal({"h": eq_hyp}, x + logos.lit(1) == logos.lit(6))

proof = run(goal, [find_equality, t.norm_num()])
print("find_equality worked:", type(proof).__name__)

find_equality worked: Refl

title: Arithmetic Tactics
text:
Arithmetic tactics prove numeric goals without requiring manual proof steps. They work by normalizing expressions and checking algebraic identities.
import logos
import logos.tactics as t

title: Arithmetic Tactics
text:
ring()
Proves polynomial ring identities over integers and rationals — commutativity, associativity, distributivity, and any combination. Internally normalizes both sides to a canonical sum-of-monomials form and checks equality.

x, y, z = logos.vars("x y z", int)

# Commutativity
logos.check(logos.prove(logos.forall(int, int, lambda x, y: x + y == y + x),
            [t.intro("x", "y"), t.ring()]))
print("x + y = y + x ✓")

# Distributivity
logos.check(logos.prove(logos.forall(int, int, int, lambda x, y, z: x * (y + z) == x * y + x * z),
            [t.intro("x", "y", "z"), t.ring()]))
print("x(y+z) = xy+xz ✓")

# Difference of squares
logos.check(logos.prove(
    logos.forall(int, int, lambda x, y: (x + y) * (x - y) == x**2 - y**2),
    [t.intro("x", "y"), t.ring()],
))
print("(x+y)(x-y) = x²-y² ✓")

# Constants fold automatically
logos.check(logos.prove(logos.forall(int, lambda x: logos.lit(3) * x + logos.lit(2) * x == logos.lit(5) * x),
            [t.intro("x"), t.ring()]))
print("3x + 2x = 5x ✓")

x + y = y + x ✓
x(y+z) = xy+xz ✓
(x+y)(x-y) = x²-y² ✓
3x + 2x = 5x ✓


ring fails when the identity doesn’t hold:

from logos.theorem import LogosProofError

try:
    logos.check(logos.prove(logos.forall(int, int, lambda x, y: x + y == x - y),
                [t.intro("x", "y"), t.ring()]))
except LogosProofError as e:
    print(f"Correctly rejected: {e}")

Correctly rejected: Proof failed: ring: Add(left=Var(name='x', type=<class 'int'>), right=Var(name='y', type=<class 'int'>)) ≠ Sub(left=Var(name='x', type=<class 'int'>), right=Var(name='y', type=<class 'int'>)) after normalization
  LHS poly: {frozenset({('x', 1)}): 1, frozenset({('y', 1)}): 1}
  RHS poly: {frozenset({('x', 1)}): 1, frozenset({('y', 1)}): -1}

title: Arithmetic Tactics
text:
linarith()
Proves linear arithmetic goals from linear hypotheses. It works by finding a non-negative linear combination of the hypotheses (Farkas’ lemma) that implies the goal.

x, y = logos.vars("x y", int)

# Simple transitive inequality
logos.check(logos.prove(
    logos.forall(int, int, lambda x, y:
        (x > logos.lit(0)) >> ((y > x) >> (y > logos.lit(0)))
    ),
    [t.intro("x", "y", "hx", "hxy"), t.linarith()],
))
print("x > 0 ∧ y > x → y > 0 ✓")

# Linear combination
logos.check(logos.prove(
    logos.forall(int, int, lambda x, y:
        (x > logos.lit(2)) >> ((y > logos.lit(3)) >> (x + y > logos.lit(5)))
    ),
    [t.intro("x", "y", "hx", "hy"), t.linarith()],
))
print("x > 2 ∧ y > 3 → x+y > 5 ✓")

# Tighter bound derivation
logos.check(logos.prove(
    logos.forall(int, lambda x:
        (x > logos.lit(10)) >> (x > logos.lit(1))
    ),
    [t.intro("x", "h"), t.linarith()],
))
print("x > 10 → x > 1 ✓")

x > 0 ∧ y > x → y > 0 ✓
x > 2 ∧ y > 3 → x+y > 5 ✓
x > 10 → x > 1 ✓








Note



linarith uses a simplified Farkas-lemma approach. It handles most common linear reasoning but may not close all valid linear goals. For those cases, try ring or break the proof into smaller steps with intro/split.

title: Arithmetic Tactics
text:
decide()
Evaluates a ground (variable-free) boolean proposition by computing its value. If it evaluates to True, the goal is closed.

# Ground numeric comparisons
logos.check(logos.prove(logos.lit(5) > logos.lit(3),  [t.decide()]))
print("5 > 3 ✓")

logos.check(logos.prove(logos.lit(2) * logos.lit(3) == logos.lit(6), [t.decide()]))
print("2 * 3 = 6 ✓")

logos.check(logos.prove(logos.lit(10) >= logos.lit(10), [t.decide()]))
print("10 ≥ 10 ✓")

5 > 3 ✓
2 * 3 = 6 ✓
10 ≥ 10 ✓


decide is most useful after instantiating universally quantified variables to specific values:

logos.check(logos.prove(
    logos.exists(int, lambda x: x * x == logos.lit(25)),
    [
        t.witness(logos.lit(5)),   # x := 5
        t.decide(),                 # verify 5 * 5 == 25
    ],
))
print("∃x: x² = 25 ✓")

∃x: x² = 25 ✓

title: Arithmetic Tactics
Tactic Combinators
text:
Combinators compose tactics into more powerful ones. They let you sequence, branch, and repeat tactics without writing a new function.
import logos
import logos.tactics as t
from logos.goal import Goal
from logos.runner import run, TacticFailed

title: Tactic Combinators
text:
then(*tactics)
Sequences tactics as a single compound tactic. Equivalent to listing them separately, but usable anywhere a single tactic is expected.

x, y = logos.vars("x y", int)

# These are equivalent:
logos.check(logos.prove(
    logos.forall(int, int, lambda x, y: x + y == y + x),
    [t.then(t.intro("x", "y"), t.ring())],   # single compound tactic
))

logos.check(logos.prove(
    logos.forall(int, int, lambda x, y: x + y == y + x),
    [t.intro("x", "y"), t.ring()],            # two separate tactics
))
print("Both work")

Both work


then is particularly useful when you need a single tactic for a subgoal:

@logos.define
def double(x: int):
    return x + x

x, y = logos.vars("x y", int)

logos.check(logos.prove(
    logos.forall(int, int, lambda x, y:
        (x > logos.lit(0)) >> ((y > logos.lit(0)) >> ((double(x) > logos.lit(0)) & (double(y) > logos.lit(0))))
    ),
    [
        t.intro("x", "y", "hx", "hy"),
        t.split(),
        [t.then(t.unfold(double), t.linarith())],  # subgoal 1 as a compound tactic
        [t.then(t.unfold(double), t.linarith())],  # subgoal 2
    ],
))
print("Proved compound goal")

Proved compound goal

title: Tactic Combinators
text:
first(*tactics)
Tries each tactic in order and succeeds with the first one that doesn’t raise TacticFailed. Useful for automation when multiple tactics might apply.

x = logos.var("x", int)

# refl works for x == x, ring works for x + 0 == x
# first tries refl, then ring

logos.check(logos.prove(logos.forall(int, lambda x: x == x),
            [t.intro("x"), t.first(t.refl(), t.ring())]))
print("x = x via first(refl, ring)")

logos.check(logos.prove(logos.forall(int, lambda x: x + logos.lit(0) == x),
            [t.intro("x"), t.first(t.refl(), t.ring())]))
print("x + 0 = x via first(refl, ring)")

x = x via first(refl, ring)
x + 0 = x via first(refl, ring)


This pattern is useful in generic automation:

# A "cheap auto" tactic that tries simple things first
cheap_auto = t.first(t.refl(), t.assumption(), t.decide(), t.ring())

logos.check(logos.prove(logos.forall(int, lambda x: x == x),
            [t.intro("x"), cheap_auto]))
logos.check(logos.prove(logos.lit(2) + logos.lit(2) == logos.lit(4),
            [cheap_auto]))
print("cheap_auto handles both")

cheap_auto handles both

title: Tactic Combinators
text:
try_(tactic)
Applies the tactic; if it fails, silently succeeds (the goal remains open for the next tactic). Never raises TacticFailed.

x = logos.var("x", int)
hyp = x > logos.lit(0)

# try_(ring()) fails silently on a non-ring goal, then assumption() closes it
logos.check(logos.prove(
    logos.forall(int, lambda x: (x > logos.lit(0)) >> (x > logos.lit(0))),
    [
        t.intro("x", "h"),
        t.try_(t.ring()),    # fails (not a ring goal), but doesn't raise
        t.assumption(),      # closes the goal
    ],
))
print("try_ worked correctly")

try_ worked correctly

title: Tactic Combinators
text:
repeat(tactic)
Applies the tactic repeatedly until it fails. Useful for stripping multiple Forall binders one at a time or simplifying iteratively.

x, y, z = logos.vars("x y z", int)

# Strip all three Foralls one at a time, then ring
logos.check(logos.prove(
    logos.forall(int, int, int, lambda x, y, z: x + y + z == z + y + x),
    [
        t.repeat(t.intro("_")),   # strips x, y, z one at a time
        t.ring(),
    ],
))
print("repeat(intro) stripped all foralls")

repeat(intro) stripped all foralls

title: Tactic Combinators
text:
all_goals(tactic)
Applies the tactic to the current goal (acts as the tactic itself). In a proof list after a splitting tactic, repeat it for each subgoal using separate nested lists, or use t.first to handle multiple cases:

x, y = logos.vars("x y", int)

# Use the same sub-tactic for each subgoal
close_with_assumption = t.assumption()

logos.check(logos.prove(
    logos.forall(int, int, lambda x, y:
        (x > logos.lit(0)) >> ((y > logos.lit(0)) >> ((x > logos.lit(0)) & (y > logos.lit(0))))
    ),
    [
        t.intro("x", "y", "hx", "hy"),
        t.split(),
        [close_with_assumption],
        [close_with_assumption],
    ],
))
print("Both subgoals closed by same tactic")

Both subgoals closed by same tactic

title: Tactic Combinators
text:
Building a Mini Automation
Combinators let you compose a simple automation tactic without writing Python:

# A mini "auto" that tries a battery of cheap tactics
auto = t.first(
    t.refl(),
    t.assumption(),
    t.decide(),
    t.ring(),
    t.linarith(),
)

# Now use it for simple goals without specifying which tactic applies
x, y = logos.vars("x y", int)

for stmt, by in [
    (logos.forall(int, lambda x: x == x), [t.intro("x"), auto]),
    (logos.lit(3) > logos.lit(2), [auto]),
    (logos.forall(int, int, lambda x, y: x + y == y + x), [t.intro("x", "y"), auto]),
]:
    logos.check(logos.prove(stmt, by))

print("mini-auto handled all three goals")

mini-auto handled all three goals

title: Number Theory
text:
Proving properties of integer arithmetic — the kind of reasoning that underlies correct numerical algorithms.
import logos
import logos.tactics as t

title: Number Theory
Number Theory
Number Theory
Number Theory
Number Theory
Number Theory
text:
Arithmetic Progressions
Sum of first n natural numbers using Gauss’s formula (as an axiom — logos doesn’t have induction in v0.2):

# Declare gauss_sum as an external function and axiomatize it
gauss_sum = logos.extern("gauss_sum", [int], int)

n = logos.var("n", int)

# Axiom: gauss_sum(n) * 2 = n * (n + 1)
@logos.axiom
def gauss_formula(n: int):
    return gauss_sum(n) * logos.lit(2) == n * (n + logos.lit(1))

# Use the axiom: from it and n > 0, we can derive gauss_sum(n) * 2 == n*(n+1)
# Here we show applying the axiom in a forall proof
logos.check(logos.prove(
    logos.forall(int, lambda n: gauss_sum(n) * logos.lit(2) == n * (n + logos.lit(1))),
    [t.intro("n"), t.apply(gauss_formula)],
))
print("gauss_sum(n) * 2 = n*(n+1) ✓")

gauss_sum(n) * 2 = n*(n+1) ✓

title: Number Theory
text:
Modular Arithmetic

# Basic mod property: (a * b) mod m = ((a mod m) * (b mod m)) mod m
# We'll prove a specific case

logos.check(logos.prove(
    logos.lit(7) * logos.lit(8) % logos.lit(5) == (logos.lit(7) % logos.lit(5)) * (logos.lit(8) % logos.lit(5)) % logos.lit(5),
    [t.norm_num()],
))
print("(7 * 8) mod 5 = (7 mod 5)(8 mod 5) mod 5 ✓")

(7 * 8) mod 5 = (7 mod 5)(8 mod 5) mod 5 ✓

title: Number Theory
Data Structures
text:
Reasoning about pure functions on data — the foundation of verified algorithm design.
import logos
import logos.tactics as t

title: Data Structures
text:
Reasoning About External Functions
When you can’t inspect a function’s source (C extensions, third-party libraries), declare it externally and axiomatize its contract:

# Declare Python's built-in sorted() as an external function
my_sorted = logos.extern("my_sorted", [list], list)

xs = logos.var("xs", list)
i = logos.var("i", int)
j = logos.var("j", int)

# Axiomatize what we know about sorted()
@logos.axiom
def sorted_length(xs: list):
    return my_sorted(xs) == my_sorted(xs)  # placeholder

# Verify concrete behavior with decide()
logos.check(logos.prove(
    logos.lit(1) < logos.lit(3),
    [t.decide()],
))
print("Ordering check: 1 < 3 ✓")

Ordering check: 1 < 3 ✓

title: Data Structures
Data Structures
Data Structures
Data Structures
Data Structures
Data Structures
text:
Ground Computation Verification
Verify that specific computations give the correct answer — essentially a proof-carrying unit test:

# Verify that our formulas compute the right values for concrete inputs
for n, expected in [(1, 1), (2, 4), (3, 9), (4, 16), (5, 25)]:
    logos.check(logos.prove(
        logos.lit(n) ** logos.lit(2) == logos.lit(expected),
        [t.norm_num()],
    ))
    print(f"{n}² = {expected} ✓")

1² = 1 ✓
2² = 4 ✓
3² = 9 ✓
4² = 16 ✓
5² = 25 ✓

title: API Reference
text: Complete reference for the logos public API.

title: API Reference
text:
Top-Level (logos)

logos.var(name, type_) → Var
Create a single symbolic variable.

x = logos.var("x", int)
print(x.name, x.type)   # x <class 'int'>

x <class 'int'>




logos.vars(names, type_) → tuple[Var, ...]
Create multiple variables from a space-separated string.

x, y, z = logos.vars("x y z", int)
print([v.name for v in [x, y, z]])  # ['x', 'y', 'z']

['x', 'y', 'z']




logos.lit(value) → Lit
Wrap a Python literal as an expression node.

from logos.expr import Lit
zero = logos.lit(0)
print(type(zero).__name__)   # Lit
print(zero.value)            # 0

Lit
0




logos.forall(type_, ..., fn) → Expr
Create a universally quantified statement from a lambda.

p = logos.forall(int, int, lambda x, y: x + y == y + x)
from logos.expr import ForallNode
print(type(p).__name__)  # ForallNode

ForallNode




logos.exists(type_, fn) → Expr
Create an existentially quantified statement from a lambda.

p = logos.exists(int, lambda x: x > logos.lit(5))
from logos.expr import ExistsNode
print(type(p).__name__)  # ExistsNode

ExistsNode




@logos.theorem — declare a theorem
Decorator that creates a Theorem object. No proof runs at declaration time.

@logos.theorem
def example_comm(x: int, y: int):
    return x + y == y + x

print(example_comm)              # Theorem[unverified]('example_comm')
print(example_comm.certified)   # False

Theorem[unverified]('example_comm')
False




thm.proof(*tactics) — attach a proof
Attach a proof spec and return self for chaining.

logos.check(example_comm.proof(t.intro("x", "y"), t.ring()))
print(example_comm.certified)   # True

True




logos.check(thm) → thm
Run the kernel. Marks thm.certified = True or raises LogosProofError.

@logos.theorem
def refl_thm(x: int):
    return x == x

logos.check(refl_thm.proof(t.intro("x"), t.refl()))
print(refl_thm.certified)   # True

True




logos.prove(statement, proof_list) → _GroundProof
Create a one-shot anonymous proof. Pass to logos.check() to validate.

gp = logos.prove(
    logos.forall(int, lambda x: x == x),
    [t.intro("x"), t.refl()],
)
logos.check(gp)
print(gp.certified)   # True

True




@logos.axiom — declare a trusted axiom
Decorator (or function call) that creates an Axiom object — trusted without proof.

@logos.axiom
def my_axiom(x: int):
    return x == x

print(my_axiom)             # Axiom('my_axiom')
print(my_axiom.certified)   # True

Axiom('my_axiom')
True




logos.define
Decorator that registers a symbolic function definition. Returns a Function object.

@logos.define
def double(x: int):
    return x + x

print(double(5))          # 10  (Python mode)
x = logos.var("x", int)
print(type(double(x)).__name__)  # App  (symbolic mode)
print(double.name)               # double

10
App
double




logos.extern(name, arg_types, ret_type) → callable
Declare an external uninterpreted function symbol.

my_fn = logos.extern("my_fn", [int], int)
x = logos.var("x", int)
from logos.expr import App
result = my_fn(x)
print(type(result).__name__)   # App

App




logos.cases(*branches) → Expr
Create a conditional expression for use inside @logos.define bodies.

x = logos.var("x", int)
expr = logos.cases(
    (x >= logos.lit(0), x),
    (x < logos.lit(0), -x),
)
from logos.expr import CaseExpr
print(type(expr).__name__)   # CaseExpr

CaseExpr

Tactics (logos.tactics)

Structural







Tactic
Description




intro(*names)
Strip Forall/Implies, add to context


assumption()
Close if goal is in context


exact(term)
Close with an explicit proof term

Apply an Axiom/Theorem object or local hypothesis string


split()
A & B → [A, B]


left(right_type)
A \| B → prove A


right(left_type)
A \| B → prove B

∃x.P(x) → P[x:=val]

Close via P and ~P in context





Arithmetic



Tactic
Description

Polynomial ring identities

Linear arithmetic via Farkas

Evaluate ground boolean propositions





Rewriting







Tactic
Description

Prove a == a

Replace function calls with definition bodies (accepts Function objects)

Tactic
Description

Kernel (logos.kernel)
The kernel is the only trusted code. Direct use is rare but available:

Proof Terms



Term
Proves

e == e

b == a (given p: a==b)

a == c (given p: a==b, q: b==c)

f(a) == f(b) (given p: a==b)


Axiom(name)
The named axiom’s statement

The named hypothesis

∀x. P

P[x:=val]

P → Q

Q (modus ponens)

A ∧ B


AndElimL(p) / AndElimR(p)

Case split

Ex falso quodlibet





Kernel Functions
from logos.kernel import infer, check, Context

# infer: determine what statement a proof term proves
stmt = infer(term, ctx)

# check: validate that term proves expected_stmt (raises KernelError on mismatch)
check(term, expected_stmt, ctx)

title: API Reference
text:
Helpers (logos.helpers)
from logos.helpers import structural_eq, substitute, free_vars

# structural_eq: recursively compare two expression trees
structural_eq(x + y, x + y)     # True
structural_eq(x + y, y + x)     # False

# substitute: replace all free occurrences of var with val
substitute(x + y, x, logos.lit(5))  # Lit(5) + y

# free_vars: names of free variables in an expression
free_vars(x + y)                 # {"x", "y"}
free_vars(Forall(x, x + y))      # {"y"}  (x is bound)
